examples.title: FCFS Disk Scheduling Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of First-Come-First-Served disk scheduling that processes I/O requests in the order they arrive
examples.code:
ALGORITHM FCFS(requests, start_head)
BEGIN
    total_seek <- 0
    current_head <- start_head
    queue <- requests  // Queue of disk addresses in arrival order
    
    WHILE queue is not empty DO
        current_request <- queue.dequeue()
        seek_distance <- ABS(current_request - current_head)
        total_seek <- total_seek + seek_distance
        current_head <- current_request
        PRINT "Serviced request at: " + current_request
    END WHILE
    
    PRINT "Total seek time: " + total_seek
    RETURN total_seek
END
examples.explanation: FCFS processes requests in arrival order without optimization. It is simple to implement and guarantees no starvation but does not minimize total seek time.

examples.title: SSTF Disk Scheduling Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of Shortest Seek Time First that selects the request closest to current head position
examples.code:
def sstf(requests, start_head):
    """
    SSTF Disk Scheduling
    requests: list of track numbers to service
    start_head: initial position of disk head
    """
    total_seek = 0
    current_head = start_head
    requests = sorted(requests)  # Sort for easier processing
    visited = [False] * len(requests)
    serviced = []
    
    while len(serviced) < len(requests):
        # Find closest unvisited request
        closest_idx = -1
        min_distance = float('inf')
        
        for i, req in enumerate(requests):
            if not visited[i]:
                distance = abs(req - current_head)
                if distance < min_distance:
                    min_distance = distance
                    closest_idx = i
        
        # Service the closest request
        visited[closest_idx] = True
        total_seek += min_distance
        current_head = requests[closest_idx]
        serviced.append(requests[closest_idx])
    
    print(f"Serviced in order: {serviced}")
    print(f"Total seek time: {total_seek}")
    return total_seek

# Example usage
requests = [98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67]
start = 53
result = sstf(requests, start)
examples.explanation: SSTF always selects the nearest request to minimize seek time. It provides better performance than FCFS but may cause starvation for distant requests.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n²) without optimization, O(n log n) with better data structures

examples.title: Bit Vector Free Space Management
examples.description: Implementation showing how bit vector tracks free and allocated disk blocks
examples.explanation: This code implements a bit vector (bitmap) for free space management. Each bit represents a disk block - 0 for free, 1 for allocated. It allows efficient tracking of free space and finding consecutive free blocks.
examples.spaceComplexity: O(n/32) bits for n blocks - very space efficient

examples.title: SCAN Disk Scheduling Algorithm
examples.description: Implementation of SCAN (elevator) algorithm that moves in one direction then reverses
examples.code:
def scan(requests, start_head, direction='forward'):
    """
    SCAN Disk Scheduling Algorithm
    requests: list of track numbers
    start_head: initial head position
    direction: 'forward' or 'backward'
    """
    total_seek = 0
    current_head = start_head
    
    # Add boundary positions (0 and max track)
    max_track = max(requests + [start_head])
    all_requests = sorted(requests + [0, max_track])
    
    # Split requests based on direction
    left = [r for r in requests if r < start_head]
    right = [r for r in requests if r >= start_head]
    
    left = sorted(left, reverse=True)  # Sort descending
    right = sorted(right)  # Sort ascending
    
    serviced = []
    
    if direction == 'forward':
        # Service all right first, then reverse to left
        for req in right:
            total_seek += abs(req - current_head)
            current_head = req
            serviced.append(req)
        
        # Go to end and reverse
        total_seek += abs(max_track - current_head)
        current_head = max_track
        
        for req in reversed(left):
            total_seek += abs(req - current_head)
            current_head = req
            serviced.append(req)
    else:
        # Service all left first, then reverse to right
        for req in left:
            total_seek += abs(req - current_head)
            current_head = req
            serviced.append(req)
        
        # Go to beginning and reverse
        total_seek += abs(current_head - 0)
        current_head = 0
        
        for req in right:
            total_seek += abs(req - current_head)
            current_head = req
            serviced.append(req)
    
    print(f"Serviced in order: {serviced}")
    print(f"Total seek time: {total_seek}")
    return total_seek

# Example
requests = [98, 183, 37, 122, 14, 124, 65, 67]
start = 53
result = scan(requests, start, 'forward')
examples.explanation: SCAN algorithm services requests in one direction until reaching the end, then reverses. This provides good performance with moderate seek time and reasonable wait time distribution.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n log n) due to sorting
